summary: Shaw reflects on a failed flower painting to argue that talent is irrelevant to enjoying creative pursuits. He describes his lack of natural ability at painting, explaining that his skills come entirely from reading books and deliberate practice rather than innate gift. He credits painting, particularly self-portraits and landscapes, with saving his life during a dark period. The core message is that having zero talent is actually liberating because it frees you to focus on the process rather than the result.
key_points: Skills that appear to be talent are often just reading books and doing what they say — including seemingly impressive feats like painting from memory
Having zero talent is liberating because it removes pressure about outcomes and lets you focus on the process
Painting self-portraits and landscapes served as therapeutic tools that helped Shaw through depression
The belief that you need talent to enjoy something prevents people from ever starting
money_quotes: I wouldn't say I have any particular talent at painting. I'm definitely not a genius and I put in a lot of work to be even moderately alright at it.
That's not talent. That's just being able to read some books and do what they say.
Everyone could learn how to do it if they put in the time and believed they could.
I could give a fuck if I have any talent or not. I'm enjoying myself and I get to give them to friends.
Self-portraits and landscape painting quite literally saved my life at a time when everything was sad and dark.
Doing paintings of my face helped me reconnect with who I am. Painting outside got me out of bed.
Having zero talent is liberating. You can stop worrying about the end result and just enjoy the process and experience.
It really doesn't matter if what you create is any good. All that matters is if you learn something for the next time.
key_insight: Talent is neither required nor desirable for enjoying a creative pursuit — liberation comes from accepting you have none and focusing entirely on the process and what you learn.
